title: Fall of Constantinople
summary: On 29 May 1453, after a fifty-three-day siege, the Ottoman sultan Mehmed II broke into Constantinople and ended the eleven-hundred-year Byzantine Empire. The last emperor, Constantine XI Palaiologos, died in the fighting. The greatest Christian city of the eastern Mediterranean became the Muslim capital of a new imperial order and, in the shorthand of many historians, the medieval world ended.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
By 1453 the Byzantine Empire had shrunk to little more than Constantinople itself and a few pockets in the Peloponnese. For a century the Ottomans had encircled it, taking Adrianople and the Balkans and using Christian vassals in campaigns from the Danube to the Euphrates. When the young sultan Mehmed II succeeded his father Murad II in 1451, at nineteen, he made the capture of the city his first great project.

He built the fortress of Rumeli Hisari on the European shore of the Bosphorus in 1452, cutting the city off from Black Sea grain, and hired the Hungarian engineer Orban to cast enormous bronze bombards. Emperor Constantine XI Palaiologos, ruling a treasury near empty, could muster perhaps five thousand Greek soldiers and around two thousand Western volunteers, most notably the Genoese condottiere Giovanni Giustiniani Longo and his professional troops.

image.caption: Portrait of Sultan Mehmed II by Gentile Bellini, 1480. National Gallery, London.
image.credit: Gentile Bellini (c. 1429 to 1507), via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The siege began on 6 April 1453. Mehmed's army of perhaps 50,000 to 80,000 men, including the elite Janissary corps, deployed against the great triple line of the Theodosian land walls, which had turned back besiegers for a thousand years. Orban's largest cannon, said to be around eight metres long and firing stone balls weighing several hundred kilograms, hammered the walls day after day. Nightly repairs by the defenders and their civilian volunteers kept the breaches closed.

A great chain across the mouth of the Golden Horn kept the Ottoman fleet out of the inner harbour, so on the night of 22 April Mehmed had some seventy of his ships hauled overland on greased log rollers from the Bosphorus into the Horn behind the chain, an audacious portage that stretched the defence dangerously thin. Small relief squadrons from Genoa broke through the blockade in April but no large Western fleet came.

Just after midnight on Tuesday 29 May the final assault began. Waves of irregulars and Anatolian regulars were thrown at the walls to exhaust the defenders; then the Janissaries went in. In the confusion at the Kerkoporta, a small postern gate near the Blachernae palace, a party of Ottoman troops entered and raised their standard on a tower. Giustiniani was mortally wounded near the Saint Romanus Gate; his withdrawal broke the defenders' nerve. Constantine XI is said to have thrown off his imperial regalia and died sword in hand in the last melee. By morning the city was Mehmed's.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Mehmed granted his troops the customary three days of plunder but reportedly cut it short. Contemporary estimates suggest around four thousand civilians died and perhaps thirty thousand were enslaved. The great church of Hagia Sophia was converted into a mosque; the sultan installed a new Greek patriarch, Gennadios Scholarios, and confirmed the Orthodox community's legal existence under Ottoman rule.

Mehmed, henceforth known as Fatih, the Conqueror, moved his capital from Edirne to the city he called Kostantiniyye and repopulated it with Muslim, Greek, Armenian, and Jewish settlers. The Ottoman Empire, now stretching from the Danube to the Euphrates, would be a decisive Mediterranean power for the next four centuries.

In Christendom the shock was profound. Refugee scholars carrying Greek manuscripts fed the Italian Renaissance, the trade routes to the East were reorganised in ways that helped push Portuguese and Spanish ships around Africa and across the Atlantic, and Moscow began to style itself the 'Third Rome', heir to a fallen Christian imperial capital. Many historians still use 1453 as the conventional boundary between the medieval and early modern worlds.

image.caption: The Hagia Sophia in Istanbul; converted from cathedral to mosque after the 1453 conquest.

worldContext.text: The Hundred Years' War ended in the same summer as the fall of Constantinople. On 17 July 1453 a French army under Jean Bureau, using field artillery to devastating effect, destroyed the last major English field force at Castillon in Gascony; the English commander John Talbot, Earl of Shrewsbury, was killed. Within weeks Bordeaux surrendered to Charles VII and English rule in France was reduced to the town of Calais, ending three centuries of Angevin and Plantagenet claims. In England the news accelerated the mental collapse of Henry VI in August 1453 and helped ignite the Wars of the Roses two years later. In central Europe the twelve-year-old Habsburg Ladislaus the Posthumous was recognised as king of Bohemia after his coronation manoeuvres of 1453; Hungary's regent Janos Hunyadi, who would soon relieve Belgrade from Ottoman siege in 1456, watched the fall of Constantinople with alarm. In the east Muscovy under Vasily II the Blind was tightening its grip over the Russian principalities and would soon claim, through his son Ivan III, the mantle of the Third Rome. Johannes Gutenberg's press at Mainz was that same year printing the folios of the forty-two-line Bible, quietly rewiring European culture.

image.caption: The death of John Talbot, Earl of Shrewsbury, at the Battle of Castillon on 17 July 1453, from Martial d'Auvergne's Vigiles de Charles VII (c. 1484). Bibliotheque nationale de France, MS Francais 5054.

worldContext.text: Ming China in 1453 was ruled by the Jingtai Emperor (Zhu Qiyu), who had taken the throne in 1449 after his elder brother the Zhengtong Emperor was captured by Esen Taishi of the Oirat Mongols at the catastrophic Battle of Tumu. The court, moved back into a defensive posture behind the reinforced walls of Beijing that the great minister Yu Qian had successfully held against Esen's siege, was recovering from a difficult year: catastrophic Yellow River flooding in the summer of 1453 devastated Henan and Shandong, and an unusually harsh winter caused widespread famine in the north. A mysterious tropical volcanic eruption in late 1452 or early 1453, later attributed to Kuwae in the New Hebrides, had begun what modern climatologists identify as a fifteen-year cold snap across the northern hemisphere. In Korea the Joseon dynasty was in the sixth year of the reign of King Danjong, whose uncle Prince Suyang would seize the throne in a coup in 1455 as King Sejo. In Japan the Ashikaga shogunate under Yoshimasa presided over a brilliant but fractious Muromachi court whose growing tensions would erupt in the Onin War of 1467 and inaugurate a century of civil war.

image.caption: The Shenwumen Gate of the Forbidden City in Beijing; the imperial complex begun by the Yongle Emperor in 1406 was, by 1453, the Ming political and ritual centre from which the Jingtai Emperor governed.

worldContext.text: The Timurid inheritance of Tamerlane, ruled from Herat by Shah Rukh until his death in 1447 and now fragmenting under his descendants, still dominated eastern Persia and Transoxiana; Ulugh Beg's great astronomical observatory at Samarkand had been razed by his son in 1449, but the scholarly tables it had produced were spreading across the Islamic world and would in time reach Kepler in Prague. Abu Sa'id Mirza, backed by the Uzbek Abu'l-Khayr Khan, was consolidating power in Transoxiana while the Qara Qoyunlu Turkmen dynasty under Jahan Shah pressed westward from Tabriz. In northern India Bahlul Lodi, an Afghan governor, had just seized the throne of the Delhi Sultanate in April 1451, founding the Lodi dynasty that would rule until Panipat in 1526; his court oversaw a modest revival of Islamic power in the Ganges plain. The Bahmani Sultanate under Alauddin Ahmad Shah II ran a rival court at Bidar in the Deccan, while the Hindu empire of Vijayanagara, ruled from a colossal capital on the Tungabhadra, dominated the south and controlled the pepper and textile trades that fed Venetian and Genoese fortunes.

image.caption: Ruins of the astronomical observatory built at Samarkand by the Timurid prince Ulugh Beg (1394 to 1449); the mural sextant was one of the largest instruments of its kind ever constructed.

worldContext.text: In West Africa the Mali Empire, whose fourteenth-century emperor Mansa Musa had famously destabilised the Cairo gold market with his hajj, was in long decline under Mansa Uli II, its provinces slipping toward independence and its trans-Saharan revenues diverted eastward. The Songhai power along the middle Niger was rising under the crown prince and future conqueror Sunni Ali, who would seize Timbuktu in 1468 and turn Gao into the greatest city of the Sahel. Timbuktu itself, home to the Sankore and Djinguereber mosques and the growing library culture that would produce the sixteenth-century Tarikh al-Sudan, remained a Malian outpost for another decade. In East Africa, the Ethiopian emperor Zara Yaqob, a scholarly monarch who had convened the Council of Debre Mitmaq in 1450 to enforce Sabbath observance, was rebuilding church discipline; on the Swahili coast, Kilwa's sultanate handled the gold trade from Great Zimbabwe. Portuguese caravels sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator had reached Cape Verde in 1444 and were feeling their way down the Atlantic coast, drawing Africa into a new maritime world.

image.caption: The Sankore Mosque and madrasa at Timbuktu, one of the great centres of Islamic learning in the Sahel during the fifteenth century.
image.credit: Dr Ondrej Havelka (HistoriaSalutis), via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: In the Valley of Mexico the Aztec Triple Alliance of Tenochtitlan, Texcoco and Tlacopan under the tlatoani Moctezuma I Ilhuicamina was in the middle of a devastating multi-year drought and famine that had begun around 1450, killing thousands, driving families to sell themselves into servitude in the Totonac lowlands, and prompting the great expansion of chinampa agriculture and aqueducts. The famine strengthened the ritual demand for captives and helped drive the Flower Wars against Tlaxcala and Huejotzingo that would define central Mexican politics until the Spanish arrival. Nezahualcoyotl, the philosopher king of Texcoco, was completing his codes of law and hydraulic projects. On the Peruvian sierra the Inca ruler Pachacuti (Cusi Yupanqui), who had defeated the Chanca around 1438 and refounded the state on new principles, was in the middle of the campaigns that would turn Cuzco's regional kingdom into the Tawantinsuyu, the largest empire of the pre-Columbian Americas, with its four quarters and its extraordinary road system stretching eventually from present-day Colombia to Chile. Elsewhere the Chimor kingdom of the Peruvian coast, centred on the vast adobe city of Chan Chan, still held its ancient tribute network but was drawing toward Inca conquest.

image.caption: Museum model of the Aztec capital Tenochtitlan showing the sacred precinct and causeways; the city under Moctezuma I in the 1450s was already the largest urban centre in the Americas.
image.credit: Steve Cadman (from London, UK), via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Mehmed II
figures.role: Ottoman sultan, aged 21, who directed the siege and became known thereafter as Fatih, the Conqueror
figures.credit: Gentile Bellini (1480), National Gallery London, Public Domain

figures.name: Constantine XI Palaiologos
figures.role: Last Byzantine emperor, killed in the final assault on 29 May 1453

figures.name: Giovanni Giustiniani Longo
figures.role: Genoese commander of the land-wall defence; mortally wounded during the final assault

figures.name: Orban
figures.role: Hungarian founder whose giant bronze bombards battered the Theodosian walls

images.caption: Mehmed II Entering Constantinople, painted by the Ottoman court artist Fausto Zonaro around the turn of the 20th century. Dolmabahce Palace, Istanbul.
images.credit: Fausto Zonaro (1854 to 1929), via Wikimedia Commons
